one_line_takeaway: VLA (robot) policies leak strong, often near-perfect membership signals even under strict black-box access to generated actions alone, and none of the five mitigations tested (noise, rounding, stochastic decoding, image jitter, MC dropout) eliminates this leakage without materially hurting task success.
key_finding: VLA models leak membership information strongly at both granularities, and critically, strict black-box attacks using only generated actions (no logits, no ground-truth token probabilities) are highly effective -- a result the paper contrasts with LLM/VLM MIA literature where black-box attacks are typically weak. Sample-level (own numbers): on pi0-fast, NLL is nearly perfect (avg AUC 0.9998, avg TPR 0.9543 at 0.1% FPR); on OpenVLA, NLL reaches avg AUC 0.8086 (up to 0.8906 on Object). Black-box Action-L1/Action-MSE reach avg AUC 0.9731/0.9604 on pi0-fast and 0.9233/0.9220 on OpenVLA, with OpenVLA TPRs of 0.7018/0.7111 at 1% FPR. The fixed-prompt confidence variant (Conf-FixPrompt) collapses to near-random, showing the original instruction context matters for that signal. Trajectory-level (own numbers): aggregation-based and black-box temporal attacks approach or reach ceiling -- e.g. on OpenVLA, Temp.-Smooth and Temp.-Curve (using only generated action sequences, no ground truth) reach avg AUC 0.9989 and 0.9993, with avg TPR 0.9725/0.9775 at 0.1% FPR; Agg.-NLL reaches AUC 1.0000 on pi0-fast across all four LIBERO suites. Ablations: reducing OpenVLA's action-token bin count from 512 to 64 raises NLL AUC from 0.7608 to 0.9133 (discretization affects likelihood-based leakage) but Action-L1 stays flat (0.9017-0.9290) across bin sizes, showing action-level leakage is a distinct channel from token-likelihood leakage. Leakage tracks fine-tuning progress: Action-L1 AUC rises from 0.5049 (no fine-tuning) to 0.8011 at 1k steps to ~0.92-0.93 by 5k+ steps, correlating with task success rate improving from 60% (1k steps) to ~70%+, implying a coupling between utility and leakage that undermines early stopping as a standalone defense. Mitigations show a real privacy-utility trade-off: output/input perturbations (Gaussian noise, action rounding, image jitter) cut leakage but sharply hurt task success; MC dropout gives the best trade-off reported (Action-L1 AUC down to 0.7216 while retaining 67.2% task success), and stochastic decoding is lighter-weight (AUC down to 0.8467, 72-74% success retained) but leaves substantial residual leakage in all cases -- no defense tested eliminates the attack without a notable utility cost.
core_contribution: The paper presents what it claims is the first systematic study of membership inference attacks (MIAs) against vision-language-action (VLA) models, i.e. robot/embodied-agent policies that map visual observations and language instructions to executable actions. The authors formalize two new inference granularities specific to VLA training data: sample-level MIA (was a single observation-instruction-action transition used in training?) and trajectory-level MIA (was a full embodied demonstration, defined via a threshold fraction rho of member transitions, used in training?). They design a suite of attacks spanning access regimes: token-probability-based attacks (action-token NLL, generation confidence, and a fixed-prompt confidence variant) and strict black-box attacks that use only the model's generated continuous actions (Action-L1 and Action-MSE distance to ground truth, plus two purely observational trajectory-level temporal signals -- action smoothness and curvature -- that need neither ground-truth actions nor token probabilities). Trajectory-level scores are also built by simple averaging (aggregation) of any sample-level score across a trajectory's timesteps.
threat_model: Adversary has query access to a deployed/fine-tuned VLA model fθ (OpenVLA, pi0-fast, and pi0.5 in an appendix) and a candidate transition or trajectory, and wants a binary membership decision. Access regimes range from white/gray-box (token probabilities plus ground-truth action, used by NLL and Conf) down to a strict black-box regime where the adversary observes only the model's generated action outputs (Action-L1, Action-MSE, and the two temporal signals), with no access to logits, weights, or gradients. The paper explicitly frames the risk as auditing/data-provenance (detecting unauthorized use of proprietary, expensive-to-collect embodied datasets) and as an individual/behavioral privacy risk (embodied trajectories can encode household routines, patient-care episodes, or other sensitive user behaviors recorded during training-data collection).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: results are drawn from representative VLA models (OpenVLA, pi0-fast, pi0.5) and the LIBERO benchmark suites only, so generalization to other robot platforms, action spaces, datasets, and real-world (non-simulation) deployments is untested. Due to the cost of pretraining-scale experiments, the study only evaluates fine-tuned models (LoRA fine-tuning on halved LIBERO splits), not full pretraining-stage membership leakage. The mitigation study is explicitly called preliminary; all five defenses tested trade off utility for privacy without eliminating leakage, and the authors state stronger VLA-specific defenses are left to future work. The trajectory-level evaluation set is small (100 member / 100 non-member trajectories per dataset), which the paper does not separately flag but is worth noting for statistical precision at very low FPR thresholds.

quantitative_results: Sample-level NLL on pi0-fast: avg AUC 0.9998, avg TPR 0.9543 @0.1% FPR
Sample-level NLL on OpenVLA: avg AUC 0.8086, up to 0.8906 on Object
Sample-level Action-L1/Action-MSE on pi0-fast: avg AUC 0.9731 / 0.9604
Sample-level Action-L1/Action-MSE on OpenVLA: avg AUC 0.9233 / 0.9220; TPR@1%FPR 0.7018 / 0.7111
Sample-level Conf on OpenVLA: avg AUC 0.8421 (up to 0.8811 on Long); Conf-FixPrompt near-random
Trajectory-level Agg.-NLL on pi0-fast: AUC 1.0000, TPR 1.0000 at all FPR thresholds, all 4 datasets
Trajectory-level Agg.-NLL/Agg.-Conf on OpenVLA: avg AUC 0.9999 / 1.0000
Trajectory-level Temp.-Smooth/Temp.-Curve on OpenVLA (black-box, no ground truth): avg AUC 0.9989 / 0.9993, avg TPR 0.9725 / 0.9775 @0.1% FPR
Ablation - action bins 64 vs 512 (OpenVLA NLL): AUC 0.9133 at 64 bins vs 0.7608 at 512 bins; Action-L1 stable at 0.9017-0.9290 across bin sizes

Ablation - fine-tuning steps (OpenVLA, LIBERO-Spatial): Action-L1 AUC 0.5049 (pre-fine-tune) -> 0.8011 (1k steps) -> 0.9125 (3k steps) -> ~0.92-0.93 (5k+ steps); task success rises from 60.0% (1k steps) to ~70% later
Mitigation - MC dropout: Action-L1 AUC reduced to 0.7216 while preserving 67.2% task success
Mitigation - stochastic decoding: AUC reduced to 0.8467 while preserving 72-74% task success
pi0.5 (Appendix A.2) sample-level Action-L1 AUC range across 4 datasets: 0.9009 to 0.9481; trajectory-level Action-L1/Agg.-Flow-Loss near 1.0 across all datasets
baselines_compared: Conf-FixPrompt (generation confidence with generic fixed prompt, no original instruction)
Action-L1 (black-box, negative L1 distance between generated and ground-truth action)
Action-MSE (black-box, negative MSE between generated and ground-truth action)
Agg.-NLL / Agg.-Conf / Agg.-Conf-Fix (trajectory-level score aggregation of sample-level signals)
Temp.-Smooth (black-box, negative first-order temporal action difference)
Temp.-Curve (black-box, negative second-order temporal action difference)
Flow-Loss / Flow-Loss-FixPrompt (pi0.5-specific likelihood substitute for flow-matching action head, Appendix A.2)
